title: Detecting Cycles in an Undirected Graph: Finding the Root

statement.markdown: Given an undirected graph with a specified root vertex, detect whether there's a cycle in the graph. If a cycle exists, print the root vertex as the starting point. If not, print 'No cycle'.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of input contains an integer n representing the number of nodes in the graph. The next n lines contain space-separated edge connections.
statement.outputFormat: If a cycle exists, print the root vertex; otherwise, print 'No cycle'.
statement.notes: This task requires you to implement a graph cycle detection algorithm using DFS or BFS. You must also identify the root vertex of the graph.

examples.explanation: A cycle exists in the graph, starting at vertex 1.

hints.text: Check if the current node has been visited before in the DFS/BFS traversal.

editorial.title: Editorial: Cycle Detection in Undirected Graph
editorial.intuition: The key to detecting cycles in a graph is to keep track of visited nodes and edges traversed during the DFS/BFS traversal.
editorial.bruteForce: Perform a brute-force traversal of the entire graph, checking for cycles at each step.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a visited set to keep track of nodes and edges encountered during traversal.
editorial.optimalSolution: Identify the root vertex and perform a depth-first search (DFS) or breadth-first search (BFS) traversal to detect cycles.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The optimal solution is proven correct by demonstrating that it identifies all cycles in the graph without false positives.
editorial.dryRun: Example usage of the optimal solution on a sample graph.
commonMistakes: Not keeping track of visited nodes

}
